api: SurveyCTO Server API v2 (https://<server>.surveycto.com/api/v2; real OpenAPI 3.0 spec published at https://developer.surveycto.com/specs/api-v2.json)
scope: Pass B full-surface expansion against the real published API v2 OpenAPI spec (22 documented paths). The v2 API is a substantially different, richer surface than the wave0/pilot bundle assumed: there is no plain GET /forms or GET /cases endpoint at all -- 'forms' are enumerated only via /forms/ids (a bare JSON array of strings, an ENGINE_GAP -- see below), and 'cases' are simply one dataset among others distinguished by discriminator: CASES in the generic /datasets catalog, not a separate endpoint. This bundle models datasets (the generic catalog, covering cases/enumerator/general datasets alike), dataset_records (fan_out per dataset), submissions (single-form-scoped, matching the only form-submissions shape the real API supports without the forms-enumeration ENGINE_GAP), groups, roles, and users as 6 read streams, plus 7 write actions (dataset CRUD, dataset record creation, user CRUD). ENGINE_GAP: GET /forms/ids and GET /teams/ids both return a bare JSON array of SCALAR STRINGS (not objects) -- connsdk.RecordsAt (used by both plain streams and fan_out's ids_from.request) only keeps array elements that decode as a JSON object, silently dropping every element of a scalar-only array; there is no dialect mechanism to fan out over, or emit records from, a bare-string-array endpoint (the same class of gap ip2whois's nameservers field hit, formalized in docs/migration/conventions.md's parity-deviation ledger item 12). This blocks both a standalone forms/teams stream AND fan_out over forms for submissions/messages-per-form; submissions is instead scoped by a required config.form_id (matching the pre-Pass-B bundle's existing working pattern) rather than iterating every form.

excluded.reason: ENGINE_GAP: response is a bare JSON array of scalar strings (e.g. ["my_survey", "registration_form"]), not objects; connsdk.RecordsAt only extracts array elements that decode as a JSON object, so every element of a scalar-only array is silently dropped -- there is no dialect mechanism (no 'array of scalars, not objects' extraction mode) to model this as a stream or as a fan_out ids_from.request source. See the deviation note in api_surface.json's scope field and docs.md Known limits.
legacy wide-format JSON export variant of form submission data (a differently-shaped export, not the standard cursor-paginated submissions list); out of scope for this pass

multipart/form-data variant for ENCRYPTED forms only (accepts an RSA private_key PEM file part to decrypt submission payloads); handling a private key file upload is a materially different shape from this dialect's JSON/form write-body model and is security-sensitive (a private key is credential-shaped data); the plain GET /submissions (non-encrypted forms) is what the submissions stream reads
POST variant of the same submissions listing GET /submissions already covers, accepting a JSON body of filters/field-selection instead of query parameters; not a distinct resource
downloads a single binary media attachment (photo/audio/video) for one submission by filename; not a JSON record read
legacy CSV-export variant of dataset data (a differently-shaped export, not the standard cursor-paginated records list); out of scope for this pass
single-dataset detail; identical record shape to the datasets list stream's per-item object, already covered by that stream
irreversibly wipes ALL records from a dataset while keeping the dataset definition itself; a bulk-erasure admin action, never a reverse-ETL-safe mutation

ENGINE_GAP: getRecord's recordId is a QUERY parameter ("passed as a query parameter to support special characters", per the API's own docs), not a path segment; engine.WriteAction has no query field at all (write.go's ExecuteWrite always calls Requester.Do/DoForm with a nil query), so a single-record-by-id lookup keyed by a query-param id cannot be expressed as either a read stream path or (see below) a write action in this dialect

ENGINE_GAP: updateRecord's recordId is a query parameter (see getRecord above); engine.WriteAction has no mechanism to templatize a query parameter on a write request, only path_fields (path segments) and the body -- this is a genuine dialect gap, not a scope-narrowing choice
ENGINE_GAP: deleteRecord's recordId is a query parameter; same gap as updateRecord above
ENGINE_GAP: upsertRecord's recordId is a query parameter; same gap as updateRecord above
POST variant of the same records listing GET /records already covers, accepting a JSON body of filters/field-selection instead of query parameters; not a distinct resource

bulk CSV/file upload of many records in one multipart request; a materially different shape from this dialect's single-record JSON write-body model (create_dataset_record already covers single-record creation)
returns a role's detailed PERMISSION structure (optionally scoped to a group), a distinct nested-object shape from the roles list stream's per-item summary, not a syncable object list of its own

ENGINE_GAP: response is a bare JSON array of scalar strings (e.g. ["team-1", "team-2"]), the identical shape/gap as GET /forms/ids above -- connsdk.RecordsAt drops every element of a scalar-only array
single-user detail; identical record shape to the users list stream's per-item object, already covered by that stream

bulk multi-user CSV/file upload in one multipart request; a materially different shape from this dialect's single-record JSON write-body model (create_user/update_user already cover single-user create/update)
same bulk multi-user CSV/file upload shape as the PUT variant above
bulk multi-user JSON array update in one request; a materially different shape from this dialect's single-record write-body model
bulk multi-user JSON array creation in one request; a materially different shape from this dialect's single-record write-body model
irreversibly bulk-deletes many users identified by a filter/list in one request; a bulk-erasure admin action distinct from the per-user delete_user action, never a reverse-ETL-safe mutation
